methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
The Red Metal Company is a seed-stage climate and energy company founded by Jackson Switzer that has raised $10M to date. The broader sector comprises nine private peers and one public comparable (Linamar, ticker LNZA), including notable companies such as Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen. However, the peer set lacks sufficient granularity—zero same-stage comparables, zero adjacent-stage peers, and zero similar-tier datapoints—to support a reliable peer-median valuation method.

The stage-progression method is applicable and forms the sole basis for triangulation. Using total raised ($10M) as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 2.5x step-up multiplier from Seed to subsequent stage yields $25M. This methodology reflects observed advancement patterns in early-stage venture capital but depends on the assumption that the company's trajectory will match historical norms for the sector.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $21M to $29M, with a median of $25M. Material uncertainty stems from the absence of narrow-set peer data; the sector contains sufficient public and private activity to justify closer examination of comparable companies if additional transaction data becomes available. Stage-progression alone cannot account for company-specific risks, technology maturity, or capital efficiency relative to peers. Investors should reassess this valuation if the company approaches its Series A with clearer performance metrics.
